turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg has our opinion in case 16-405, BNSF Railway versus Tyrrell.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Under the Federal Employers Liability Act, FELA, railroads are liable in money damages to their employees for on-the-job injuries. The two cases we decide today concern where such suits may be brought. Robert Nelson and Brent Tyrrell both worked for BNSF Railway and both were injured on the job. Though neither worker resided in Montana or was injured there, Nelson and Tyrrell's wife, the administrator of his death estate, each brought FELA suits against BNSF in Montana State Courts. The Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment limits the jurisdictional reach of state courts. In Daimler v. Bauman, we held that state courts may exercise general or all-purpose personal jurisdiction over an out-of-state corporation, that is, jurisdiction when the claim in suit arose elsewhere, only if the defendant is at home in the state. Two paradigm forums in which a corporation is at home are at its place of incorporation and principal place of business. Exceptionally, there may be other at-home forums, states where the corporation's affiliations are so substantial and of such a nature as to make it answerable in the state's courts no matter where the episode in suit took place. Incorporated in Delaware and having its principal place of business in Texas, BNSF Railway moved to dismiss Nelson's and Tyrrell's suits on the ground that it is not at home in Montana. In Nelson's case, the railroad's motion was granted by the court of first instance. In Tyrrell's, the motion was denied. The Montana Supreme Court, consolidating the two suits, ruled that an FELA provision, 45 U.S.C §56, allows Montana courts to exercise personal jurisdiction over railroads doing business in the State. BNSF, with over 2,000 miles of track and 2,000 employees in Montana, the Court concluded, qualified as doing business there. The due process constraint announced and applied in Daimler, the Court added, did not control because Daimler did not involve and FELA claim or a railroad defendant. We reverse the Montana Supreme Court's judgment. Section 56 of the FELA, the Provision on which the Montana Supreme Court relied, has nothing to do with personal jurisdiction. It is first relevant sentence states that, “An action may be brought in the United States District Court in, among other places, the district in which the defendant shall be doing business at the time of commencing such action.” The sentence is a venue prescription governing proper locations for FELA suits filed in federal court. It does not address suits filed in state court. The second relevant sentence of §56 states, “The jurisdiction of the cause of the United States under this chapter shall be concurrent with that of the courts of the several states.” That sentence refers to subject matter jurisdiction, not personal jurisdiction. It simply clarifies, that not only federal courts but state courts too, can entertain FELA lawsuits. The Montana Supreme Court also erred on the constitutional boundaries for exercises of state court jurisdiction. The due process constraint described in Daimler applies generally. It does not vary based on the type of suit or the character of the corporate defendant sued. The Montana Court's exercise a personal jurisdiction over BNSF Railway here was incompatible without decisions on the due process constraint. BNSF is not incorporated nor headquartered in Montana, and its contacts with the State, while no doubt more than minimal, are not so substantial as to render it at home there. Justice Sotomayor has filed an opinion concurring in part and dissenting in part
